text: Code

Objective
Learn the essential PyTorch concepts needed to implement BERT: tensors, autograd, nn.Module, and DataLoader.

Learning Outcomes

Understand PyTorch tensors and operations
Master automatic differentiation (autograd)
Build custom neural network modules
Use DataLoader for efficient batch processing

Estimated Time
2 hours

Reading Material

PyTorch Documentation
Deep Learning with PyTorch

What You’ll Build
By the end of this project, you will have implemented:

✅ PyTorch Fundamentals - Tensors, autograd, nn.Module
✅ Self-Attention Mechanism - Query, Key, Value
✅ Multi-Head Attention - Parallel attention heads
✅ Transformer Encoder - Residual connections, layer normalization
✅ Positional Embeddings - Sinusoidal and learned
✅ BERT Architecture - Embeddings + Encoder + Pooler
✅ Pretraining - Masked Language Modeling (MLM) + Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)
✅ Finetuning - Classification and Question Answering
✅ Inference & Serving - REST API with FastAPI
✅ Production Optimization - ONNX export, quantization

Prerequisites

Python 3.10+
Basic understanding of neural networks
Familiarity with linear algebra (matrix operations)
PyTorch basics (covered in Task 1)

How to Use This Project

Start with Task 1 - Each task has a Jupyter notebook with explanations
Implement the code - Follow along and write your own implementations
Complete exercises - Test your understanding with exercises
Check the reference - API docs for the modular code

Quick Start
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/autokernel.git
cd autokernel

# Create virtual environment
python -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate  # or venv\Scripts\activate on Windows

# Install dependencies
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Launch Jupyter
jupyter notebook
Then navigate to tasks/task-01-pytorch-basics/notebook.ipynb to begin!

Project Structure
autokernel/
├── tasks/           # Learning modules (12 tasks)
│   └── task-*/      
│       ├── notebook.ipynb
│       └── overview.md
├── src/             # Modular Python code
│   └── bert/
├── exercises/       # Additional exercises
├── tests/           # Unit tests
└── _site/          # Built documentation

Why Build BERT From Scratch?

Deep Understanding - Go beyond high-level APIs
Interview Prep - Master transformer internals
Customization - Modify architecture for your needs
Research - Build on top for novel contributions

Let’s begin! 🚀

Masked Language Modeling (MLM)
Randomly mask 15% of tokens and predict them: - 80% replaced with [MASK] - 10% replaced with random token - 10% kept as original

title: Task 7: BERT Pretraining
text:
Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)
Predict if sentence B follows sentence A (IsNext or NotNext).

title: Task 2: Self-Attention Mechanism
text: Self-attention is the fundamental building block of transformers. It allows each position in a sequence to attend to all positions in the previous layer.

title: Task 2: Self-Attention Mechanism
text:
What You’ll Learn

Query, Key, Value - The three vectors used in attention
Scaled Dot-Product Attention - The core attention computation
Attention Weights - How to visualize what the model attends to
Why Scaling - Understanding the scaling factor

title: Task 2: Self-Attention Mechanism
text:
Theory: How Self-Attention Works
Given an input sequence, self-attention computes:
Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k))V
Where: - Q (Query): What I’m looking for - K (Key): What I can offer - V (Value): What I actually give - d_k: Dimension of keys (for scaling)

title: Task 2: Self-Attention Mechanism
section: 1. Creating Q, K, V from Input
text: 1. Creating Q, K, V from Input

title: Task 2: Self-Attention Mechanism
text: 2. Scaled Dot-Product Attention

title: Task 2: Self-Attention Mechanism
text: 3. Why Scale? The Gradient Problem

title: Task 2: Self-Attention Mechanism
text: 4. Self-Attention as a PyTorch Module

title: Task 2: Self-Attention Mechanism
text:
Exercises

Exercise 1: Implement Self-Attention from Scratch
Implement self-attention WITHOUT using any PyTorch operations except basic matrix ops (matmul, transpose). Use only torch.matmul, torch.transpose, torch.softmax, and basic math operations.


Exercise 2: Masked Attention
Implement attention with a causal mask (so each position can only attend to previous positions). This is used in decoder-only models like GPT.


Exercise 3: Multi-Token Attention Visualization
Create a longer sequence (e.g., 10 tokens) and visualize how attention learns to attend to different positions. Use different random seeds and observe patterns.

title: Task 2: Self-Attention Mechanism
text:
Summary
You learned: - ✅ Query, Key, Value concept and projections - ✅ Scaled dot-product attention computation - ✅ Why scaling by sqrt(d_k) is important - ✅ How to implement self-attention as a PyTorch module - ✅ Visualizing attention weights

title: Task 3: Multi-Head Attention
text: Multi-head attention allows the model to attend to different representation subspaces simultaneously.

title: Task 3: Multi-Head Attention
text:
What You’ll Learn

Why Multiple Heads? - Different heads learn different relationships
Multi-Head Implementation - Parallel attention computation
Concatenation & Projection - Combining head outputs

title: Task 3: Multi-Head Attention
text:
Theory: Multi-Head Attention
Multi-head attention applies \(h\) attention heads in parallel:
MultiHead(Q,K,V) = Concat(head_1, …, head_h)W^O
where head_i = Attention(QW_i^Q, KW_i^K, VW_i^V)
Each head can learn different types of relationships!

title: Task 3: Multi-Head Attention
text:
Exercises

Exercise 1: Implement Multi-Head from Single-Head
Build multi-head attention by combining multiple SingleAttention instances.

title: Task 3: Multi-Head Attention

✅ Concatenation followed by linear projection
✅ Parallel computation is efficient

title: Task 1: PyTorch Foundations
text:
Exercises
Now it’s time to practice what you’ve learned!

Exercise 1: Tensor Manipulation
Create a 3D tensor of shape (2, 3, 4) filled with random values. Then: - Reshape it to shape (2, 12) - Transpose it so the shape becomes (4, 3, 2) - Slice it to get the first element of the first dimension


Exercise 2: Custom Linear Layer
Implement a custom Linear layer with: - Xavier/Glorot initialization for weights - Optional bias term - Test it with a forward pass


Exercise 3: Training Loop
Create a custom Dataset for a classification problem (e.g., binary sentiment: positive/negative). Train a simple MLP to classify the data.

title: Task 1: PyTorch Foundations
text:
Summary
In this task, you learned: - ✅ PyTorch tensors and basic operations - ✅ Automatic differentiation with autograd - ✅ Building custom modules with nn.Module - ✅ Using DataLoader for batch processing - ✅ Training loops with optimizers
